coredata.dc:title: Paper-based sensor for visual detection of Ag+ based on a “turn-off-on” fluorescent design
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Hengye
Hu, Ou
Fu, Haiyan
Fan, Yao
Xu, Lu
Meng, Qingjun
Zhang, Lei
Lan, Wei
Wu, Chunying
Tang, Siyi
She, Yuanbin
coredata.dc:description: A fluorescence paper-based sensor method by simply using thiomalic acid modified CdTe (TMA-capped CdTe) was proposed for visual detection of silver ions (Ag+). Red-emission TMA-capped CdTe with a large amount of carboxyl groups on the surface could be combined with Ag+ by electrostatic interaction, thus leading to the fluorescence quenching. It was found that the adding of amino acids contributed to fluorescence recovery by the leaving of Ag+ on the surface of TMA-capped CdTe. Moreover, the more amino group, benzene and thiol group equipped in the structure of amino acid, the stronger fluorescence restoring of CdTe could be achieved. This fluorescence spectrometry method for Ag+ showed low detection of limit with the value of 13.16 nmol/L, with no response to other heavy metals, which demonstrating its high sensitivity and high selectivity for Ag+. More importantly, this paper-based sensor method combined with chemometrics could accurately identify and quantify different concentrations of Ag+ in different complex matrices. This fast, economical, accurate and effective approach exhibited a new idea for the on-site detection of Ag+, and was expected to be extended to the on-site detection of Ag+ in biological and environmental fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Quantum dots
Ag+
Chemometrics
Paper-based sensor
Visual detection

Sensitivity and selectivity of the assay for Ag+
CdTe/Ag+ as turn-on sensor platform for amino acids

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

The paper images for TMA-capped CdTe upon titration of Ag+ ions in aqueous solution ([TMA-capped CdTe] = 23.7 μmol/L).
Comparison of the detection limit of QDs-based sensing for Ag+ detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The concentration of Ag+ and Cu2+ were all 1 μmol/L; The concentration of other metal ions were all 10 μmol/L; The concentration of TMA-capped CdTe was 1.46 μmol/L; The concentration of Tris–HCl buffer was 50 mmmol/L (pH = 7.5).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of Ag+ in human plasma, bovine serum, lake water and tea water.
A detailed splitting information of Ag+ in matrices with different concentrations (0–1000 nmol/L).

head.ce:title: Paper-based sensor for visual detection of Ag+ based on a “turn-off-on” fluorescent design

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Modernization Engineering Technology Research Center of Ethnic Minority Medicine of Hubei Province, School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, South-Central University for Nationalities, Wuhan 430074, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: cCollege of Material and Chemical Engineering, Tongren University, Tongren 554300, Guizhou, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Hengye Chen and Ou Hu equally contributed to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A fluorescence paper-based sensor method by simply using thiomalic acid modified CdTe (TMA-capped CdTe) was proposed for visual detection of silver ions (Ag+). Red-emission TMA-capped CdTe with a large amount of carboxyl groups on the surface could be combined with Ag+ by electrostatic interaction, thus leading to the fluorescence quenching. It was found that the adding of amino acids contributed to fluorescence recovery by the leaving of Ag+ on the surface of TMA-capped CdTe. Moreover, the more amino group, benzene and thiol group equipped in the structure of amino acid, the stronger fluorescence restoring of CdTe could be achieved. This fluorescence spectrometry method for Ag+ showed low detection of limit with the value of 13.16 nmol/L, with no response to other heavy metals, which demonstrating its high sensitivity and high selectivity for Ag+. More importantly, this paper-based sensor method combined with chemometrics could accurately identify and quantify different concentrations of Ag+ in different complex matrices. This fast, economical, accurate and effective approach exhibited a new idea for the on-site detection of Ag+, and was expected to be extended to the on-site detection of Ag+ in biological and environmental fields.

ce:section.ce:para: Thousands of tons of silver ions (Ag+) are released into the environment through industrial waste and emissions every year. Through the accumulation of food chains, people can directly or indirectly consume foods and medicines contaminated by heavy metal. Ag+ is designated as the most toxic and dangerous heavy metal contaminant [1], and because of its extensive application in industry, it poses a serious threat to the environment and human health. It is well known that Ag+ can induce the generation of inactive thiol enzymes, and also bind to carboxyl, aminesand imidazolesgroups of various metabolites such as proteins with high molecular weight and metallothioneins, which do serious harm to human body [2,3]. Daily intake of Ag+ can cause anemia, heart enlargement, growth retardation and degenerative changes in animals [4]. Human exposed to Ag+ can trigger blood and urine silver excretion. Conventional analysis technologies, like inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) and atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy [5,6], have been used to detect of trace heavy metals in water. However, these techniques require expensive instruments and complex sample preparation processes, resulting in limited application [5,6]. In contrast, spectroscopic methods, especially fluorescence spectroscopy, show unique potentials such as high selectivity, high sensitivity, and simple operation [7]. Fluorescence sensing methods based on “turn-off” or “turn-on” model have been widely employed for the detection of heavy metal ions [8]. Therefore, the selection of a suitable fluorescent probe is particularly important for the detection of heavy metal ions with high specificity and sensitivity.

Fluorescent nanomaterials have attracted the interest of researchers because they can be used in different fields such as chemical sensors, biosensors and biological imaging [10–12]. Among the fluorescent probes studied, quantum dots (QDs), as a kind of semiconductor nanocrystals, are obtained increasing attention due to its good stability, high biocompatibility, high quantum yield and low cytotoxicity [14]. Furthermore, the detection of heavy metals based on quantum dot fluorescence sensor method is widely used [15–18], such as diethyldithiocarbamate-modified CdSe/CdS quantum dots were used as a fluorescent probe for the detection of Cu2+ [19], water-soluble CdSe quantum dot was employed for the detection of Cr3+ [20], and 3-mercaptopropionic acid (3-MPA)-capped CdSe quantum dots was designed to detect Cu2+ [21]. The functionalized quantum dot fluorescent probe was also widely used in the detection of Ag+. For example, mercaptoacetic acid modified CdSe quantum dots was reported to selectively detect silver ions with the limit of detection (LOD) of 7.0 × 10−8 mol/L [22]. Thioglycolic acid (TGA) modified CdTe was highly selective to Ag+ A new strategy for high-selectivity detection of Ag+ was developed by using the red-shift of emission wavelength of CdTe quantum dots modified with thioglycolic acid (TGA), in which the LOD was 5.0 × 10−8 mol/L [23]. Gan et al. selectively detected Ag+ using the synthesized mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) capped CdTe as fluorescence probe with the LOD of 4.106 × 10−8 mol/L [24]. These researches demonstrated that the carboxyl group had interaction with Ag+, which was beneficial to the detection of Ag+. However, the LOD of these methods for Ag+ were not low enough and relied on large instruments fluorescence spectrophotometer. However, the detection of Ag+ based on a paper-based sensor method successfully solved the problems. The groups of Chumbimuni-Torres developed a novel method for the detection of Cd2+, Ag+, K+ using paper-strip ion-selective electrode, the LOD of Ag+ was as low as 25.1 nmol/L [25]. However, this strategy had the disadvantages of high cost and complicated preparation. Therefore, it is very important and necessary to develop a simple, inexpensive and high-sensitivity alternative for the on-site detection of Ag+.

In this work, a method was developed for detecting Ag+ based on thiomalic acid capped CdTe (TMA-capped CdTe) fluorescence sensor. The experimental results showed that there was a good linearity between the fluorescence intensity of QDs and the concentration of Ag+. And other metal ions hardly caused changes in the fluorescence intensity of CdTe, proving the high sensitivity and high selectivity to Ag+. Compared with the performance of detecting Ag+ by CdTe modified by TGA and MPA, the LOD of this method can be low to 13.16 nmol/L. The method has been successfully applied to the detection of Ag+ in complex water and biological matrices. Moreover, this approach provided new alternatives for sensing different amino acids. More importantly, this strategy had been successfully applied in the paper-based sensors for the detection of Ag+, realizing the aim of simple, rapid, inexpensive, and on-site detection of Ag+. Moreover, the paper-based sensor was also highly sensitive and highly selective to Ag+, and could be applied to the detection of Ag+ in complex water and biological matrices.

Cadmium chloride (CdCl2), sodium borohydride (NaHB4), hydrochloric acid were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Mercaptosuccinic acid (TMA) was obtained from Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. Serine and sodium citrate (Na2TeO3) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. 3-hydroxymethyl-methane (Tris) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. Metal ions (Hg2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, K+, Zn2+, Ba2+, Na+, Al3+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Ag+, Ni2+) were all prepared from their salt-forming compounds and were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Human plasma and bovine serum were obtained from Zhejiang Tianhang Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Zhejiang, China). The lake water was from Nanhu Lake in Wuhan, China, which was filtered through a 0.45 μm microporous membrane. Longjing tea was purchased at a local supermarket. Quantitative filter papers were purchased from Hangzhou Wohua Filter Paper Co., Ltd. All solutions were prepared from fresh deionized distilled water obtained from the Molgeneral 220a water purification system (Molecular).

The fluorescence spectra were acquired by scanning with an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi High-Tech Company, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on a Thermo Fisher Nicolet 6700-FT-IR spectrometer (USA). UV–vis spectra were recorded by a UV–vis Spectrophotometer (UV-1800 PC) with the baseline correction. The morphology and particle size of the quantum dot CdTe were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, TECNAI G2 20 S-TWIN, USA). The obtained round paper chip was obtained by cutting a laser engraving machine (Shandong Xinbang Co., Ltd.). All images on paper were taken by an iPhone 6 camera. The obtained photos were simulated by Photoshop software to extract RGB values. Both partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLSDA) and partial least squares regression (PLSR) methods were performed by Matlab software (MathWorks, USA) for data processing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of TMA-capped CdTe quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: TMA-capped CdTe QDs were synthesized based on the reported method with some modification [24]. 0.1142 g (12.5 mmol/L) of CdCl2 and 0.0901 g (15 mmol/L) of TMA were added to 40 mL of ultrapure water, stirring at room temperature for 15 min. Then its pH was adjusted to 11.00 with sodium hydroxide solution. Afterwards, the mixture was stirred under a nitrogen atmosphere in an ice bate for 20 min. 0.0216 g (2.5 mmol/L) of Na2TeO3 and 0.0113 g (7.5 mmol/L) of NaHB4 were added into the mixture and stirred for 15 min. Finally, the solution was heated at 200 °C in a reaction still, then moved to a drying oven of 200 °C for 50 min. TMA-modified CdTe fluorescent probe (λem = 620 nm) was obtained, when it cooling to room temperature.

The paper-based analytical devices were constructed through immobilization of prepared TMA-capped CdTe solutions on paper. Firstly, the quantitative filter paper was cut into several circular paper sheets with a diameter of 6.0 mm by using a laser engraving machine. And then, these circular paper sheets were neatly separated on the hydrophobic double faced adhesive tape, which ensured each circular paper sheet being non-interfering and independent. Afterwards, as-prepared 10 μL of TMA-capped CdTe solutions were added on the circular paper. Each circular paper was slightly dried in an oven at 37 °C for 5–7 min. Finally, different concentrations of Ag+ aqueous solution with 10 μL were dropped on to the circular papers and reacted at room temperature for 4 min. The color responses of the circular papers were observed by the naked eyes under a 365 nm ultraviolet analyzer and taken pictures by telephone camera.

According to the results of instrument characterization of TMA-capped CdTe (Fig. S1), it had good fluorescence stability (Fig. S1A); the FT-IR spectroscopy showed that TMA-capped CdTe were abundantly modified by carboxyl (Fig. S1B); and its particle size was about 4 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. S1C). And the spectral peaks of 679, 996, 1406 & 1577, 2920 and 3385 in FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1B) represent the CS, CO, C
O, CH and OH bonds of TMA, respectively. To get high-performance fluorescence quenching, the following parameters were optimized: reaction time and pH. First, to evaluate the kinetics of the TMA-capped CdTe fluorescent probe for Ag+, the fluorescence quenching rate (F 0/F 1) was measured after the addition of 1 μmol/L Ag+ at different reaction times. F 0 and F 1 were used to represent the fluorescence intensities of the fluorescent probe without and with the addition of Ag+, respectively. As shown in Fig. S2A, the fluorescence quenching rate of the fluorescent probe after the addition of 1 μmol/L Ag+ for 3 min was optimal, indicating that the reaction was a very quick process [26]. Therefore, the 4 min being in the plateau was chosen as its incubation time. Secondly, different pH values (pH = 6.0, 6.5, 7.0, 7.5, 8.0, 8.5, 9.0) of the system were studied. The result showed(Fig. S2B) that the fluorescence intensity of the TMA-capped CdTe fluorescent probe was stable at different pH values; the fluorescence quenching intensity of TMA-capped CdTe by Ag+ starkly increased with the pH value at 7.5. The acid or basic condition was not best for the reaction. Therefore, we chose Tris–HCl buffer with pH = 7.5 as the medium for Ag+ detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and selectivity of the assay for Ag+
ce:section.ce:para: Under the optimal assay conditions mentioned above, the sensitivity of the present method for Ag+ were then evaluated. The K sv value were determined by the fluorescence quenching rate (F0/F1) and the concentration of Ag+. Among them, F0 and F1 represented the fluorescence intensity of the TMA-capped CdTe without or with the addition of Ag+, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3, different concentrations of Ag+ were added into the fluorescent probe. The fluorescence intensity of TMA-capped CdTe quantum dots decreased with the increase of Ag+ concentration. The inset in Fig. S3 clearly showed that the fluorescence quenching rate (F 0/F 1) was in a good linearity with the concentration range of Ag+ in the range of 80–400 nmol/L (y = 0.91138 + 0.00139CAg+, R = 0.9966), and the detection of limit (three times of the standard deviation of background) was as low as 13.16 nmol/L. Toxic levels of Ag+ in drinking water is 460 nmol/L, which defined by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA). Furthermore, the quenching behavior of the TMA-capped CdTe by Ag+ followed the Stern-Volmer equation: F 0/F 1 = 1 + K sv[M]

In this equation, K sv represented the Stern-Volmer constant, and the value of K sv reflected the sensitivity of the sensing system [27], and [M] was the concentration of Ag+. From the slope of the linear equation, the K sv value for Ag+ was calculated to be 1.18 × 106 mol/L. It was obvious that the TMA-capped CdTe was highly sensitive to Ag+. Compared with other reported methods based on quantum dot fluorescence probes (Table 1 ), our method for Ag+ had a relatively low detection of limit.

In order to appraise the selectivity of TMA-capped CdTe fluorescent probes for Ag+ detection, effects of various other metal ions (Hg2+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Na2+, K+, Ni2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Cu2+) were respectively examined. The results of Table 2 indicated that there was almost no response to TMA-capped CdTe fluorescent probes in the presence of other metal ions, whereas these metal ions in the presence of Ag+ could lead to obvious fluorescence quenching. It was demonstrated that the TMA-capped CdTe fluorescent probes was highly selectivity to Ag+ and the interference of other metal ions hardly affects the detection of Ag+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CdTe/Ag+ as turn-on sensor platform for amino acids
ce:section.ce:para: Researches have been reported that Ag+ had a strong affinity to nitrogen donors in serum albumin [34]. Therefore, we further explored the turn-on behaviors of CdTe/Ag+ after the addition of different kinds of amino acids. Serine, as the ubiquitous amino acids in serum albumin, was chosen to explore the needed time of fluorescence recovery after quenching. The ratio of fluorescence recovery intensity (F2) to fluorescence quenching intensity (F1) was used as the fluorescence recovery rate. After quenching the TMA-capped CdTe by Ag+ with a fixed concentration of 1 μmol/L, serine with the concentration of 0.1 μmol/L was added and incubated for different time. As shown in Fig. S4A, the F 2/F 1 value remained nearly constant after 2 min. In the further experiments, CdTe/Ag+ solution added with amino acids was incubated for 2 min. Different concentrations of serine were added into the CdTe/Ag+ solution under conditions of 1 μmol/L Ag+ and 2 min incubation time. As shown in Fig. S4B, the fluorescence recovery rate (F2/F1) had a good linearity with the concentrations of serine in the range of 0–200 nmol/L (y = 1.00498 + 0.00406 Cserine, R = 0.9979), when the concentration of serine increased to 300 nmol/L, the fluorescence recovery intensity no longer enhanced. These results indicated that the TMA-capped CdTe/Ag+ sensing platform could be used for the quantitative detection of serine. The fluorescence recovery behaviors of the TMA-capped CdTe/Ag+ system after the addition of other amino acids were shown in Fig. S5. The results showed that all these amino acids could restored the fluorescence of TMA-capped CdTe/Ag+, and the restoring capability ranged as follows: (L-Ser, l-Glu, l-Thr) < (L-Lys, L-His, L-Pro) < (L -Phe, L-Tyr) < (NL-Cys, L-Cys). Among them, L-Ser, L-Glu and L-Thr both had an amino group and a carboxyl group, and L-Lys, L-His and L-Pro contained two amino groups and one carboxyl group, and both L-Phe and L-Tyr contained an amino group, a carboxyl group and a benzene ring, while both N-L-Cys and L-Cys have an amino group, a carboxyl group and a thiol group. These results indicated that the more amino groups the amino acids had, the better fluorescence recovery effect was; and the presence of benzene ring and sulfhydryl group was beneficial to the fluorescence recovery of TMA-capped CdTe/Ag+ system. It was further illustrated that these electron-donor groups participated in the fluorescence recovery of the system and were related to their electron-supplying capabilities.

To better understand the detection process, the possible detection mechanism was discussed. The UV absorbance spectra of TMA-capped CdTe were recorded in the absence or presence of Ag+ in the range of 80–400 nmol/L. As shown in the Fig. S6, the presence of Ag+ or the increasing of its concentration did not cause either significant spectral shift or intensity variation of the UV spectra. These results reflected that the fluorescent probe did not form complexes with Ag+. In addition, the unconventionally large K sv value of 1.18 × 106 mol/L for the Ag+ mean that the possibility of a dynamic quenching mechanism was ruled out. However, a similar phenomenon was discovered by Chattopadhyay and his teammates, who summarized that the effective electrostatic interaction between positively charged metal ions and negatively charged surfactants was responsible for the large K sv value [35]. Therefore, we speculated that the electrostatic interaction between positively charged Ag+ and TMA-capped CdTe enriched in electron-supplying carboxyl groups played an important role for the high sensitivity of Ag+ detection [36]. The results of Fig. S1B also proved that there were significant carboxyl groups in TMA-capped CdTe. And the high specificity of Ag+ detection mainly comes from the steric hindrance formed by the carboxyl group of TMA [37]. The reason why the addition of serine to TMA-capped CdTe/Ag+ could restore fluorescence was due to the presence of both carboxyl and amino groups in serine, and the binding ability of Ag+ to amino groups was stronger than that to carboxyl groups, which caused the fluorescence recovery of TMA-capped CdTe/ Ag+. The mechanism of the entire sensing system was shown in Scheme 1 .

The practicability of this TMA-capped CdTe in detecting Ag+ in real samples was further investigated. Different concentrations of Ag+ in different complex matrices (human plasma, bovine serum, lake water, and green tea water) were examined by using this method. The result showed that (Table 3 ) the recoveries of Ag+ were in the range of 98.5–104.3% for the human plasma, bovine serum, lake water and tea water, with relative standard deviation (RSD) less than 1.1% (n = 3). These results proved that this fluorescence sensor could satisfy the practicability for the detection of Ag+ in complex biological matrices and environmental water.

In the practice application, the serious pollution of heavy metal ions expands the requirement of the rapid, accurate and instrument-free detection methods. Therefore, we further applied this method in the paper-based sensors to achieve the purpose of simple, rapid, sensitive and on-site detection. The TMA-capped CdTe fluorescent probe was dropped on the paper chip, and the paper chip were slightly dried, which came into being the TMA-capped CdTe substrates of paper chips. Then Ag+ was added dropwise on the substrate, and the visual inspection was carried out after the 2–3 min incubation in the ultraviolet black box. First, we optimized the concentration of TMA-capped CdTe on paper chips. As shown in Fig. S7, the paper chip substrate with four different concentrations of CdTe (17.8 μmol/L, 23.7 μmol/L, 35.5 μmol/L, and 71.0 μmol/L) were respectively reacted with 1 μmol/L Ag+. Among these paper chips, the paper chip with 23.7 μmol/L had the most obvious color change. The 23.7 μmol/L CdTe was chosen as the optimal concentration of paper chip substrate. Then, we explored the sensitivity of paper chips for Ag+. As shown in Fig. 1 , the addition of different concentrations of Ag+ to the TMA-capped CdTe-based paper sensor produced significant degrees of color changes. As the concentration of Ag+ increased, the color of paper chips changed from deep red to pink, then to reddish, and finally to purple. The PS software was used to extract the RGB values of each paper chip, and color simulation of the paper chips were performed, showing the same trend of color changes. The selectivity of the paper-based sensor for Ag+ was further investigated. As shown in Fig. S9, the prepared TMA-capped CdTe-based paper chips had almost no color change to other ions, whereas it had obvious fluorescence quenching effect causing by Ag+. This reflexed that paper-based sensor based on TMA-capped CdTe had good selectivity for silver ions. The addition of different concentrations of serine to the paper chip based on TMA-capped CdTe/Ag+ still allowed fluorescence to restore. The color of the paper changed from purple to reddish, and then to deep red along with the concentration increasing of serine (Fig. S8). This phenomenon was also consistent with the results of fluorescence spectroscopy experiments, clearly demonstrated that the TMA-capped CdTe-based paper sensor method was a rapid, portable, high-selectivity, high-specificity, and instrument-free analysis tool for Ag+.

Further, we further investigated the applicability of paper-based sensor for Ag+ in real samples (human plasma, bovine serum, lake water, and tea water). The paper-based sensors were added with the solutions of Ag+ (100–1000 nmol/L) in different complex matrices, leading to significant degrees of color changes. In order to further realize the rapid and accurate identification of different concentrations of Ag+ in different complex matrices, Matlab software was employed to convert paper images into RGB color values and to construct data matrix. First, the partial least squares discriminant analysis (PLSDA) was employed to identify the different concentrations of Ag+ in five complex matrices (ultrapure water, human plasma, bovine serum, lake water, and tea water). Each matrix contained six different concentrations of Ag+ (0 nmol/L, 100 nmol/L, 400 nmol/L, 600 nmol/L, 800 nmol/L, 1000 nmol/L) and a total of 60 samples were obtained for each matrix (10 samples per concentration). Sixty samples were randomly divided into training sets and prediction sets (Table 4 ). The dummy codes of the six concentrations of Ag+ samples were f01(1,0,0,0,0,0), f02(0,1,0,0,0,0), f03(0,0,1, 0,0,0), f04(0,0,0,1,0,0), f05(0,0,0,0,1,0), f06(0,0,0,0,0,1). All samples were recognized based on the location of the largest dummy code. The PLSDA discrimination results were shown in Table S1. Different concentrations of Ag+ in different matrices can achieve 100% recognition rate in both training set and prediction sets. And their sensitivity and specificity were calculated as 1 [38]. These results indicated that the different concentrations of Ag+ in matrices could be discriminated with high sensitivity and high specificity based on RGB color data combined with the PLSDA model.

We further used the PLSR model to link the RGB color values of paper chips extracted from different complex matrices to the concentration of Ag+. In order to obtain a more robust and low-complexity model, the number of the latent variables (LVs) was determined by obtaining lower root mean squared errors according to eightfold cross validation. The optimal LVs for PLSR models in ultrapure water, human plasma, bovine serum, lake water, and green tea water were selected as 7, 8, 6, 6, and 6, respectively. The root mean squared error of the calibration (RMSEC) and the root mean squared error of the prediction (RMSEP) in the training set and the prediction set were used to estimate the accuracy of the calibration models. The coefficients of determination R 2 c and R 2 p were used to evaluate the relevant relationships between the predicted concentration and the actual concentration in the training set and the prediction set, respectively. The prediction results of different models were shown in Table S2. The correlation coefficients between the actual concentration and the predicted concentration of the training set and the prediction set were all greater than 0.9994, and the root mean squared error did not exceed 9.0544. These results indicated that the established PLSR model based on color RGB value was fully applicable to the prediction of Ag+ concentration in complex matrices and provided an accurate and reliable quantitative analysis method for Ag+.

In summary, we successfully synthesized a TMA-modified CdTe fluorescent probe and applied it to the detection of Ag+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. The fluorescence spectroscopy combined with paper-based sensor method could both achieve the detection of Ag+ with high selectivity and sensitivity. Furthermore, the TMA-capped CdTe/Ag+ could be used as turn-on sensor platform for different amino acids. The carboxyl group attached to the surface of the TMA-capped CdTe could bind to Ag+ by electrostatic interaction along with its fluorescence quenching. The amino and carboxyl groups of the added amino acid could simultaneously bind to Ag+. The latter binding force was stronger than the interaction between Ag+ and TMA-capped CdTe, so that Ag+ left the surface of TMA-capped CdTe and QDs fluorescence restored. Paper-based sensor was also successfully applied to the detection of Ag+ in complex biological matrices and environmental water, which provided a more rapid, simple, economical, accurate method for the visual detection of Ag+, and based on the chemical and thermal stability of quantum dots, this paper-based sensor will have better storage stability than the biosensor. So this new strategy exhibited great potential to perform visual analysis in the chemical, biological and environmental fields.

Hengye Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - review & editing. Ou Hu: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Haiyan Fu: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision. Yao Fan: Writing - original draft. Lu Xu: Formal analysis. Qingjun Meng: Resources. Lei Zhang: Formal analysis. Wei Lan: Resources. Chunying Wu: Investigation. Siyi Tang: Investigation. Yuanbin She: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grants nos. 31972164, 21776321, 21665022, 21706233), Key Projects of Technological Innovation of Hubei Province (2016ACA138), Guizhou Provincial Science and Technology Department (Nos. QKHJC[2017]1186, QKHZC[2019]2816, and QKHPTRC[2020]5009), the Talented Researcher Program from Guizhou Provincial Department of Education (QJHKYZ[2018]073), and Tongren science and Technology Bureau (No. TSKY2019-3), the Talented Youth Cultivation Program from “the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities”, and South-Central University for Nationalities (No. CZP20007).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.microc.2020.104887.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Gold nanoparticles and cleavage-based dual signal amplification for ultrasensitive detection of silver ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Miao, L. Ning, X. Li, Gold nanoparticles and cleavage-based dual signal amplification for ultrasensitive detection of silver ions, Anal. Chem.85 (2013) 7966–7970.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X.B. Zhang, Z.X. Han, Z.H. Fang, G.L. Shen, R.Q. Yu, 5, 10, 15-Tris (pentafluorophenyl) corrole as highly selective neutral carrier for a silver ion-sensitive electrode, Anal. Chim. Acta562 (2006) 210–215.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Chen, C.Y. Liu, D.Y. Tsai, Y.C. Yeh, A portable fluorescence resonance energy transfer biosensor for rapid detection of silver ions, Sensors Actuat. B-Chem.259 (2018) 784–788.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silver selective electrodes based on methyl-2-pyridyl ketone oxime, phenyl-2-pyridyl ketone oxime and bis [2-(o-carboxythiophenoxy) methyl]-4-bromo-1-methoxybenzene carriers

Niknam, Silver selective electrodes based on methyl-2-pyridyl ketone oxime, phenyl-2-pyridyl ketone oxime and bis [2-(o-carboxythiophenoxy) methyl]-4-bromo-1-methoxybenzene carriers, Sensors Actuat. B-Chem.96 (2003) 669–676.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.R. Taitt, G.P. Anderson, F.S. Ligler, Evanescent wave fluorescence biosensors: advances of the last decade, Biosens. Bioelectron.76 (2016) 103–112.
